pattern: Counting how many numbers up to n have a 1 in a specific decimal place is a closed-form function of that place's higher digits, current digit, and lower digits, so summing over every place gives the total without ever iterating over the numbers themselves.
hints: Instead of scanning every number from 0 to n, count contributions place by place: for the units place, then the tens place, and so on, working out how many numbers have a 1 sitting in that exact place.

Split n around the place you're counting into a 'higher' part, the single 'current' digit at that place, and a 'lower' remainder; the count of 1s contributed by that place depends only on whether the current digit is less than, equal to, or greater than 1.

If the current digit is 0, that place contributes higher * place_value; if it's exactly 1, it contributes higher * place_value + (lower + 1); otherwise it contributes (higher + 1) * place_value -- sum this over every place value (1, 10, 100, ...) up to n.

edge_inputs.description: a run of nines just below a power of ten
a number whose own digits include several ones
a six-digit boundary value well past a power of ten

stress.description: random n across a moderate range, checkable against a literal digit-count brute force
stress.note: kept small so the literal brute force stays fast

stress.description: random n up to the real constraint
stress.note: exercises the closed-form counting at realistic scale
